title: Jobs and spending data put rate rise back in play
full_text:
The jobless rate has dropped to 4.4 per cent and household spending has jumped higher than expected, in a sign the economy may not be slowing enough to ease high inflation, supporting a case for more interest rate increases by the Reserve Bank.
The Australian Bureau of Statistics said household spending rose 1.3 per cent in May, reversing a 1.1 per cent decline in April. The result was much higher than the 0.5 per cent economists had expected and was driven by spending on hotels, cafes, restaurants and clothing.
The economy added 40,300 jobs (about 5000 full-time and 35,000 part-time), which was stronger than the 32,500 expected by economists.
AMP deputy chief economist Diana Mousina said that the 4.4 per cent unemployment rate was still low despite rising this year and household spending had rebounded strongly - a "hawkish" signal for the RBA.
It comes after the RBA's nine voting members unanimously held the cash rate at 4.35 per cent this month, pausing a cycle of three consecutive increases of 0.25 of a percentage point this year.
"Today's data is a signal that conditions in the economy are still inflationary. Jobs growth is holding up relatively well and the unemployment rate is low, which is consistent with ongoing solid wages growth," Mousina said.
"Despite consumers saying that they feel concerned about the outlook and cost-of-living challenges, their spending habits tell a different story."
The 1.3 per cent rise in household spending was the fifth-highest monthly jump in three years. Mousina noted there was strong growth in non-essential spending on clothing, restaurants and retail shopping - as consumers took advantage of early end-of-financial-year sales.
"After a short moderation last month, spending has quickly rebounded back to the normal pattern," she said.
The surge was partly driven by airlines providing fewer refunds to travellers cancelling their trips from the war in Iran, but it was still higher than economists expected at 0.6 per cent when those are excluded.
The jobless rate is tracking above the RBA's forecasts in May, which expected 4.2 per cent in June and 4.3 per cent in December.
But an unemployment rate of 4.4 per cent is still low by historical standards, and it is near the RBA's estimates of the rate the economy can sustain while not fuelling higher or lower inflation.
"This is a very welcome reminder of the strength of our labour market and the resilience of our economy in the face of all this global uncertainty," Treasurer Jim Chalmers said in a statement.
The ABS said on Wednesday that the trimmed mean inflation rate - which strips out the most volatile price movements and is the RBA's preferred measure - ticked up from 3.4 to 3.6 per cent in May.
It was 3.3 per cent in February, which was the start of the conflict between Iran and the United States. Inflation was already too high before the conflict, prompting the first interest rate rise before the war began.
"The steady labour market and strong household spending data released today point to resilience in domestic demand," EY chief economist Cherelle Murphy said. "With supply constraints related to the Middle East conflict yet to be fully felt, upside risks to inflation remain."
Economists warned the latest inflation data this week would boost chances of a fourth rate rise later this year. However, monthly readings of the unemployment rate are notoriously volatile and so a month's worth of data is unlikely to shift the RBA's thinking significantly.
The RBA's next board meeting is on August 11, but the central bank's governor, Michele Bullock, warned in June that unless inflation came down, growth would have to slow and unemployment would have to rise.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Luke Kinsella
heading: Banking and Economy
